did_this_paper_use_ai_to_obtain_results_.details: The paper uses a hybrid deep learning model combining Vision Transformers (ViT) for dermoscopic image feature extraction and Multi-Layer Perceptrons (MLP) for processing clinical metadata to classify skin lesions (benign vs. malignant) using the ISIC 2019 dataset.

is_this_paper_a_review_paper_or_a_resear.details: The paper presents original experimental work proposing a hybrid deep learning model (Vision Transformer and Multi-Layer Perceptron) for skin lesion classification. It details the methodology, implementation on the ISIC 2019 dataset, and reports specific quantitative results (accuracy, F1-score, precision, recall), which characterizes it as a research paper rather than a review.

title: Skin Lesion Classification Using Vision Transformers and Clinical Metadata
journal: 2025 5th International Conference on Emerging Research in Electronics, Computer Science and Technology (ICERECT)
brief_summary: This study proposes a hybrid deep learning model, SkinLesionNet, that combines Vision Transformers (ViT) for dermoscopic image feature extraction and Multi-Layer Perceptrons (MLP) for processing clinical metadata to classify skin lesions as benign or malignant. The model was trained and evaluated on the ISIC 2019 dataset, which includes 25,331 dermoscopic images and associated patient metadata such as age, sex, and anatomical site. The evaluation approach utilized late fusion of visual and non-visual data, with performance measured using accuracy, precision, recall, and F1-score on a binary classification task. The hybrid model achieved an overall accuracy of 87% and an F1-score of 0.92 for benign lesions, outperforming unimodal baselines. The results demonstrate that integrating clinical metadata significantly improves diagnostic reliability, particularly for malignant cases, by providing essential context for ambiguous lesions. The system was implemented in PyTorch and deployed as a Streamlit web application for real-time clinical triage.

all_task_types.target: Binary classification of skin lesions into benign or malignant categories.
all_task_types.details: The paper explicitly states the task is 'Skin Lesion Classification' and 'classification of skin cancer'. The model predicts discrete diagnostic categories (benign vs. malignant) using late fusion of Vision Transformer image features and Multi-Layer Perceptron clinical metadata. Performance is measured using accuracy, precision, recall, and F1-score on a binary classification task. The output is a probability score via sigmoid activation used for binary classification.

datasets_used.name: ISIC 2019 Dataset
datasets_used.description: A multimodal dataset from the International Skin Imaging Collaboration (ISIC) 2019 Challenge, containing dermoscopic images and structured patient metadata (age, sex, anatomical site).

use.training: Used for training the hybrid Vision Transformer and Multi-Layer Perceptron model.
use.validation: Used for validation to monitor convergence and prevent overfitting.
use.testing: Used for final evaluation of classification performance (accuracy, precision, recall, F1-score).

statistical_methods_used.method: Five independent training runs with distinct random seeds
statistical_methods_used.purpose: To ensure robustness and assess model stability by measuring the variance in accuracy across runs.
To quantify the stability of model performance, finding that accuracy did not vary by more than ±1.2%.
To evaluate the contribution of clinical metadata by measuring the drop in malignant recall (6.2%) and increase in false negatives (4.1%).
To assess bias by comparing prediction outputs between subgroups defined by sex and age.

hypothesis_testing.interpretation: The paper did not report formal hypothesis testing (e.g., p-values or significance tests). It reported performance metrics (accuracy, F1-score, etc.) and robustness checks (accuracy variance across five runs) but did not perform statistical hypothesis testing to compare models or validate significance.

Baseline Models (CNN-only, ViT-only, Mod.Note: SkinLesionNet outperformed these unimodal or baseline fusion models, though specific metric values for baselines were not explicitly reported in the text.

ISIC 2019 Dataset.notes: The model was evaluated over five independent training runs with distinct random seeds; accuracy did not vary by more than ±1.2%. An ablation study removing the MLP branch resulted in a 6.2% drop in malignant recall and a 4.1% increase in false negatives. The dataset consisted of 25,331 images, with the test set containing 3,800 samples (82% benign, 18% malignant). The system was deployed as a Streamlit web application with processing time < 2 seconds. Fairness evaluation across sex and age subgroups showed no significant differences in prediction outputs.

architecture_used.main_model: SkinLesionNet (Hybrid Vision Transformer and Multi-Layer Perceptron)
architecture_used.pretraining: Not explicitly reported; images normalized by ImageNet statistics.
architecture_used.input_size: 224 x 224 pixels for dermoscopic images; structured metadata (age, sex, anatomical site).

architecture_details.vision_branch: Vision Transformer (ViT) for feature extraction from dermoscopic images. Image is segmented into patches, flattened, and processed via self-attention mechanisms to capture global spatial features.
architecture_details.metadata_branch: Multi-Layer Perceptron (MLP) for processing structured clinical metadata. Age is z-score normalized; sex and anatomical site are embedded. Features pass through multiple fully connected layers with ReLU activation and dropout regularization.
architecture_details.fusion_strategy: Late fusion. Outputs (embeddings) from the ViT and MLP branches are concatenated into a common feature vector.
architecture_details.classifier_head: Dense layers with sigmoid activation to produce a probability score for binary classification (benign vs. malignant).

classification_or_output_head.output: Probability score for benign or malignant classification

machine_learning_or_deep_learning_models: SkinLesionNet (Hybrid Vision Transformer and Multi-Layer Perceptron)
preprocessing_techniques: Dermoscopic images resized to 224 x 224 pixels
Data augmentation applied to malignant samples only
Categorical metadata (sex and anatomical site) embedded

data_augmentation_techniques.details: Applied to malignant samples only during training.

conclusions: The proposed hybrid deep learning model, SkinLesionNet, which combines Vision Transformers (ViT) for dermoscopic image feature extraction and Multi-Layer Perceptrons (MLP) for clinical metadata processing, achieved an overall classification accuracy of 86.69% (reported as 87%) on the ISIC 2019 dataset.
The model demonstrated superior performance compared to unimodal baselines (CNN-only or ViT-only), with an F1-score of 0.92 for benign lesions and 0.56 for malignant lesions.

Integrating clinical metadata (age, sex, anatomical site) significantly improved classification outcomes, particularly for rare or ambiguous cases, by providing essential context that visual data alone lacked.
An ablation study confirmed the importance of the metadata branch, showing that removing the MLP branch resulted in a 6.2% drop in malignant recall and a 4.1% increase in false negatives.
The model exhibited stable performance across five independent training runs, with accuracy varying by no more than ±1.2%.
Fairness evaluation across subgroups defined by sex and age showed no significant differences in prediction outputs, mitigating potential bias.
The system was successfully deployed as a Streamlit web application with a processing time of less than 2 seconds, demonstrating viability for real-time clinical triage.

The study acknowledges that the model's performance on malignant cases was lower than on benign cases due to class imbalance (18% malignant vs. 82% benign in the test set), indicating a need for improved sensitivity to malignant lesions.
important_remarks: The study relies exclusively on the ISIC 2019 dataset, which introduces potential population and dataset bias as it is a single-source collection; external validation on diverse, multi-center datasets is required to confirm generalizability.

The model exhibits significant performance disparity between classes, with a malignant recall (sensitivity) of only 0.48 compared to a benign recall of 0.95, indicating a high rate of false negatives for malignant lesions due to class imbalance (18% malignant vs. 82% benign).

Data augmentation was applied to malignant samples only, which may artificially inflate performance metrics for the minority class or create distributional shifts not present in real-world clinical settings.

The paper does not report formal statistical hypothesis testing (e.g., p-values) to validate the significance of performance improvements over baselines, relying instead on accuracy variance across five runs (±1.2%) as a robustness check.
The architecture complexity is noted as higher than simpler networks, potentially limiting deployment in resource-constrained clinical environments despite the reported <2 second processing time.

The fairness evaluation across sex and age subgroups was limited to checking for significant differences in prediction outputs rather than comprehensive bias mitigation strategies, and the specific metrics for this evaluation are not detailed.

The study acknowledges that visual inspection of misclassified images revealed misleading features often overlaid with metadata, suggesting potential data leakage or artifact dependency in the training data that could affect real-world reliability.
